programs.name: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)
OPI-M (Medicaid-funded)

eligibility.age: 18 or older (60+ or 18-59 with physical disability meeting Social Security standards)
eligibility.income_limits: Up to 400% of Federal Poverty Level (FPL), which adjusts annually in January. In 2025: $5,217/month for individual. For couples: $5,637/month (2022 figure; only applicant's income counted, spouse's disregarded). No spousal income allowance.[1][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: Up to equivalent of six months of Medicaid nursing facility costs (exact dollar amount not specified in sources; higher than standard Medicaid long-term care programs). Specific counts/exemptions follow OSIPM non-standard living arrangements rules in OAR chapter 461.[2][1][5]
other_requirements: Oregon resident.
Assessed at Service Priority Levels 1-18 (functional impairment in ADLs: transferring, mobility, eating, toileting, bathing, dressing; no Nursing Facility Level of Care required).[1][3][5]

For ages 18-59: Disability meeting OAR 461-125-0370 (Social Security standards); excludes intellectual/developmental disabilities, or mental/emotional/substance use disorders unless specific criteria met.[5][4]
Live in own home or with loved one (not assisted living, residential care, or adult foster homes).[6]
Not receiving other Medicaid LTSS like SPPC, APD Waiver, 1915(i/j/k) state plan services, Healthier Oregon, or duplicative OPI/OAA services.[1][5]
Continuous 24-month coverage even if income/assets change.[1]

benefits.value: In-home and community-based long-term services and supports including adult day care, assistive technology, emergency response systems, home modifications, and supports for family caregivers. Specific hours/dollar amounts not detailed; scaled to assessed needs.[2][7][8]

methods: Contact local Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) or ECM for assessment (no specific phone/website listed; start via Oregon DHS seniors-disabilities services). Process involves CA/PS assessment, SPL determination, MED disability check if applicable, then OPI-M application sent upon approval.[7]
No specific online URL, phone, mail, or in-person details in sources; administered by Oregon Department of Human Services (DHS).[4][7]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned (entitlement-like with continuous coverage, but assessment-based).
forms: OPI-M application (sent after initial assessment/SPL approval); follows OSIPM non-MAGI processes.[7][4]
documents: Proof of income, assets, residency, disability (if 18-59), ADL needs assessment via CA/PS.[4][7]

geography.regional_variations: Administered locally via ADRC/ECM; potential differences in providers, offices, wait times not detailed.
offices_or_providers: Local Aging and Disability Resource Connections (ADRCs), Elderly and Disabled Care Managers (ECMs).

gotchas: Cannot receive simultaneously with other Medicaid LTSS (e.g., SPPC, APD Waiver); must choose OPI-M.[1][5]
Excludes under-60 with intellectual/developmental disabilities or certain mental health/substance issues unless exceptions met.[5]
Only own-home settings; no assisted living.[6]
Income/assets higher than standard Medicaid LTC but still capped; 24-month lock-in helps but verify annually.[1]
Requires SPL 1-18 assessment first; not automatic.[3][5]
For couples, only applicant income counts but no spousal allowance.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Eligibility via tiered Service Priority Levels 1-18 based on ADL needs; higher income/asset thresholds than other Oregon Medicaid LTC; 24-month continuous eligibility lock-in; disability determination only for under-60; assessment-driven services via local ECM/ADRC.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits or financial requirements for PACE enrollment. Medicaid eligibility covers costs fully; non-Medicaid participants pay private monthly premiums. For context, Oregon long-term care Medicaid (not required for PACE) has income under $2,901/month (300% FBR) and assets $2,000 or less (excluding primary home).[1][4][7]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits for PACE enrollment. Medicaid-related asset rules apply only if seeking Medicaid coverage ($2,000 limit, excluding primary home).[1][4][7]
other_requirements: Reside in a PACE program's approved service area in Oregon upon enrollment.
Assessed as needing Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC), equivalent to Oregon Service Priority Level (SPL) 1-13 under OAR chapter 411, division 15.
Able to live safely in a community setting (home, assisted living, or CBC facility) with PACE services without jeopardizing health/safety of self or others.
Medicaid eligible or willing to pay private fees.
Willing to receive all health and long-term care exclusively from PACE providers.
Not eligible if: active enhanced care or long-term care at Oregon State Hospital; physician-documented Medicare skilled care condition unlikely to discharge to community within 30 days.

benefits.value: All-inclusive Medicare and Medicaid-covered services plus long-term care: primary medical care, prescriptions, adult day services at health/social centers, in-home services, occupational/physical therapy, social work, dietician support, medical equipment/supplies, CBC facility care, nursing home care (if enrolled), palliative care. No deductibles/co-pays for PACE-provided services. Person-centered plan of care by interdisciplinary team (doctors, nurses, therapists, aides, etc.). Services at PACE centers or home/community.

methods: Contact PACE organization intake specialists for screening (e.g., Providence ElderPlace: service areas Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop, Tillamook counties).
Phone/website via Oregon Department of Human Services (DHS) Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) at 1-855-673-2372 or local AAA.
In-person at PACE health/social centers.
application.waitlist: Possible; varies by PACE provider and region (no statewide data).
forms: No specific form name/number listed; intake screening by PACE organization.
documents: Proof of age (ID/birth certificate).
Residency proof in service area.
Medical records/assessment for NFLOC (SPL 1-13).
Medicaid/Medicare status if applicable.
Financial info if paying privately.

geography.restrictions: Limited to PACE provider service areas (e.g., Providence ElderPlace: Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop, Tillamook counties). Not available statewide.
geography.regional_variations: Services/providers limited to certified PACE organizations; availability, wait times, premiums vary by location. Fewer rural providers.
offices_or_providers: Providence ElderPlace (Portland area: Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop, Tillamook counties).
Other DHS-approved PACE organizations via local AAA/ADRC.

gotchas: Must live in specific PACE service area; relocation to area may be required.
Exclusive care from PACE providers only—no outside services allowed.
NFLOC assessment (SPL 1-13) required despite community living goal.
Private pay premiums for non-Medicaid (amounts not specified, vary by provider).
Enrollment denial only for inability to live safely in community (appealable); other issues like hospital stays may delay.
Not statewide—check local availability first.
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset test for enrollment; restricted to few PACE provider service areas in Oregon (e.g., Portland region); NFLOC via SPL 1-13; private pay option; exclusive provider lock-in.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP)

income_limits.description: Oregon MSP has four tiers with different income limits. Income limits are updated annually in March. All figures below are current as of 2025-2026.

tiers.program: Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB/SMB in Oregon)

tiers.note: Part B premiums only; enrollment capped by federal funding
For disabled workers under 65 who lost Social Security disability benefits due to substantial gainful employment but retain Part A eligibility

important_notes: Oregon uses gross income as the starting point, then subtracts $20 from income. Additional deductions apply if there is earned income.
Families may still qualify even if income appears to exceed limits due to these deductions.
Income limits increase each year in March.

asset_limits.description: No asset limits for Medicare Savings Programs in Oregon.
asset_limits.note: This is a significant advantage compared to some other states and programs.

other_requirements: Must be enrolled in or eligible for Medicare Part A (hospital insurance benefits)
If eligible for any other Medicaid benefit offered by the State of Oregon, you are NOT eligible for the SMF (QI) program
Must be a resident of Oregon

covers: Medicare Part A and Part B premiums, deductibles, and co-insurance

benefits.value: Varies by program tier; QMB provides most comprehensive coverage including all premiums and cost-sharing; SLMB and QI cover Part B premiums only

methods.contact: Local state Aging and People with Disabilities office
methods.note: Contact via phone number above to locate nearest office

methods.contact: Contact local Aging and People with Disabilities office for specific procedures
methods.note: Oregon Department of Human Services website: oregon.gov/odhs

waitlist.description: QI/SMF program has enrollment caps. Oregon is only allowed to serve a certain number of people each year for the SMF benefit. Enrollment must shut down if the limit is reached.
waitlist.note: QMB, SLMB, and QMB-DW do not appear to have enrollment caps

forms.note: Official Oregon DHS form for MSP application

documents: Proof of Medicare Part A enrollment or eligibility
Proof of income (recent pay stubs, tax returns, Social Security statements, etc.)
Proof of residency in Oregon
Identification
Note: Specific document requirements should be confirmed by contacting local office

geography.restrictions: Oregon-wide program; must be Oregon resident
offices_or_providers: Administered through local state Aging and People with Disabilities offices throughout Oregon
Multnomah County has a specific program called 'Oregon Medicare Savings Connect' with same eligibility criteria
Contact 1-800-282-8096 to locate your local office

gotchas: QI/SMF program has annual enrollment caps and may close to new applicants if funding limit is reached — apply early if interested in this tier
If someone qualifies for ANY other Medicaid benefit in Oregon, they are ineligible for the SMF (QI) program — this is a common disqualifier
Income limits are updated annually in March; families should reapply or verify eligibility each year
Oregon's income calculation starts with gross income and subtracts $20, plus additional deductions for earned income — families may qualify even if they think their income is too high
No asset limits in Oregon means savings and resources don't affect eligibility, unlike some other states
QMB-DW is specifically for disabled workers under 65 who lost Social Security disability benefits due to work but retained Part A eligibility — this is a narrow but important category
The program helps with cost-sharing but does NOT cover Medicare Part D (prescription drugs) — separate assistance may be available through other programs
Processing time is not publicly specified; families should contact their local office for timeline expectations
programs.data_shape_notes: Oregon MSP is structured as a four-tier income-based program with no asset limits. The key distinction is that QMB covers comprehensive cost-sharing while SLMB and QI cover only Part B premiums. QMB-DW is a separate track for a specific disabled worker population. The QI/SMF program has enrollment caps that can close mid-year, making it time-sensitive. Income calculations use gross income with specific deductions, meaning stated limits may not be hard cutoffs. All income limits update annually in March.

programs.name: SNAP Food Benefits (Oregon)

eligibility.age: No minimum age requirement for household members, but special rules apply for households with members 60 or older

income_limits.description: Gross income limit is 200% of federal poverty level (Oct. 1, 2025 - Sept. 30, 2026)[1]. Households with members 60+ or with disabilities can qualify under Net Income and Asset tests if they exceed gross income limits[1].
income_limits.note: Oregon has expanded eligibility beyond standard federal requirements[1]

asset_limits.general_rule: Your house, car, or money in the bank usually don't count against eligibility[2]

eligibility.citizenship_requirements: Must be U.S. citizen or eligible non-citizen[2]. Undocumented non-citizens are not eligible[4]
other_requirements: Must live in Oregon[2]
Able-bodied adults without children may need to meet work requirements to receive benefits for more than three months (ABAWD status)[2][7][9]
Work rules apply to adults ages 18-64 without disability or without children under 14[9]
Household definition: people you live with and buy food with, including spouse and children under 22[1]

benefits.value: Benefit amounts based on household's net income. General formula: $100 more in net income = $30 less in benefits[1]. Minimum and maximum benefit amounts apply but specific amounts not detailed in search results[1]
special_provisions_for_seniors: Adults 60+ can have out-of-pocket medical costs counted to offset income, potentially qualifying for more benefits (proof required)[2][5]
Adults 65+ can have full shelter costs included when determining benefits[2]
Can use SNAP at community meal sites like Meals on Wheels[6]
Can order groceries online at Amazon and Walmart[6]
If household is all 65+ or living with SSI/SSDI disability, benefits issued as cash for flexibility[6]

methods: Online: Through state SNAP agency (specific URL not provided in search results)
Phone: Contact Oregon Department of Human Services (specific number not provided in search results)
In-person: Local SNAP office (specific locations not provided in search results)
Mail: Application can be submitted by mail (specific address not provided in search results)
application.processing_time: If approved for expedited benefits, can receive within 7 days[3]. Standard processing time not specified in search results
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned in search results
forms: Standard SNAP application form (specific form number not provided)
Alternate payee form (if designating someone to purchase food on behalf of applicant)[2][5]
documents: Proof of out-of-pocket medical costs (if 60+ or disabled, to claim medical deductions)[2][5]
Proof of income
Proof of citizenship or eligible non-citizen status[2]
Information about household members and who buys/prepares food together[1]
expedited_benefits_eligibility: Monthly income and cash total less than rent/mortgage and utilities combined[3]
Monthly income under $150 and less than $100 in cash/bank accounts[3]
Farmworker status with less than $100 in cash and no other resources[3]

geography.restrictions: Work requirements now enforced statewide as of recent policy change[7]

regional_variations.direct_deposit_check_ebt_card_availabili: Adults 65+ in Clackamas, Columbia, Multnomah, and Washington counties can receive benefits as direct deposit, check, or EBT card[2][5]
regional_variations.cash_benefits: If all household members are 65+ or living with SSI/SSDI disability, benefits issued as cash in some regions[6]

geography.offices_or_providers: Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) administers program; specific office locations not provided in search results

gotchas: Only about half of eligible seniors participate in SNAP despite qualifying[3] — many don't realize they're eligible
Households with members 60+ have different eligibility pathways: can fail gross income test but still qualify via Net Income and Asset tests[1]
Medical cost deductions require proof — families must gather documentation of out-of-pocket medical expenses to maximize benefits for seniors[2][5]
Work requirements now apply statewide; about 37,000 Oregonians were immediately affected by recent policy change[7]
Household definition matters: if elderly parent lives with adult child, they may be counted as one household even if they buy food separately[1]
Alternate payee form allows trusted person to purchase food on behalf of senior, but form must be completed and submitted[2][5]
Oregon has expanded eligibility beyond federal standards, so eligibility may differ from other states[1]
Expedited benefits (7-day approval) available but must be specifically requested at application[3]
SNAP can be used at Meals on Wheels and other community meal sites, not just traditional grocery stores[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Benefits scale by household size and net income using a formula rather than fixed tiers. Special rules for households with elderly (60+) or disabled members create multiple eligibility pathways. Oregon's expanded eligibility means income limits are higher than federal baseline. Regional variation exists only for payment method options (direct deposit/check/EBT vs. cash) and is limited to specific counties. Work requirements recently expanded statewide, affecting able-bodied adults 18-64 without disabilities or dependent children.

Regional variation exists only for payment method options (direct deposit/check/EBT vs. cash) and is limited to specific counties. Work requirements recently expanded statewide, affecting able-bodied adults 18-64 without disabilities or dependent children."
```json
}
```

## Key Takeaways for Families

**For elderly loved ones specifically:** Oregon's SNAP program has **special pathways for seniors 60+**[1][2]. Even if an elderly person's income exceeds the gross income limit, they can still qualify by meeting the Net Income and Asset tests instead[1]. This is a critical distinction many families miss.

**Medical cost deduction is the biggest benefit multiplier:** If your elderly relative is 60+, you can deduct their out-of-pocket medical expenses from their income when calculating benefits[2][5]. This often qualifies them for significantly more assistance. Gather receipts and documentation for medications, medical equipment, and healthcare costs.

**Work requirements now apply statewide:** As of recent policy changes, able-bodied adults ages 18-64 without disabilities or children under 14 must meet work requirements to receive benefits beyond three months[7][9]. This affects mixed-age households.

**Application details are limited in official sources:** The search results don't provide specific phone numbers, website URLs for online applications, or local office addresses. Contact the Oregon Department of Human Services directly at their main website (https://www.oregon.gov/odhs/food/pages/snap.aspx) to get current contact information and application methods[2].

**Expedited benefits available:** If your family needs food assistance immediately, ask about expedited SNAP when applying — approval can happen within 7 days if you meet specific income/cash thresholds[3].

programs.name: Low Income Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household income must be at or below 200% of federal poverty level[1][4]. Income is calculated as gross household income (all income before payroll deductions) for all household members[1]. Specific dollar amounts vary by household size and are set annually by the U.S. Department of Energy[5]. The search results do not provide the complete 2026 income table, but reference materials indicate these limits are published by USDOE[5].
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available search results
other_requirements: Applicants can be renters or homeowners[4]
Can live in mobile homes or site-built homes[4]
Households receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) are also eligible[6]

benefits.value: Free weatherization and energy conservation services including: ceiling, wall, and floor insulation; energy-related minor home repairs; air infiltration reduction; furnace repair and replacement; heating duct improvements; and energy conservation education[1][5]
benefits.priority_tiers: Priority is given to: seniors (60 years of age and older), people with disabilities, households with children under age six, high residential energy users, and households with high energy burden[1]

methods: Contact your local Community Action Agency (CAA) by phone[5]
County-specific phone numbers: Clackamas County, Marion/Polk County, Multnomah County, Washington County, Yamhill County[5]
For Lane County: Contact Homes for Good[7]
For Mid-Willamette Valley area: Contact Mid-Willamette Valley Community Action[8]
Portland residents may also contact Community Energy Project for additional no-cost energy efficiency upgrades[5]
application.processing_time: Not specified in search results. Households are placed on a weatherization waiting service list after initial contact[1]
application.waitlist: Yes, households may be placed on a weatherization waiting service list after making initial contact with their local service agency[1]. Specific wait times not provided in search results
forms: Application form[3]
Weatherization Supplemental form[3]
Declaration of Household Income form[3]
documents: Copy of electric bill[3]
Copy of gas bill (if applicable)[3]
Proof of identification for all household members over 18: Driver's License, Passport, Military ID, State ID/Government issued ID, Birth Certificate, or Social Security Paperwork[3]
Copy of mortgage statement, deed, or manufactured structure ownership document (must verify homeowner name and street address)[3]
For wage income: copies of paycheck stubs for 60 days prior to application date[3]
For Social Security, SSDI, or SSI: current year benefit verification letter[3]

geography.restrictions: Program operates statewide through local Community Action Agencies and other community-based organizations[1]
geography.regional_variations: Different counties have different local administrators. Specific providers include: Clackamas County, Marion/Polk County, Multnomah County, Washington County, Yamhill County (through their respective CAAs)[5]; Lane County (Homes for Good)[7]; Mid-Willamette Valley area (Mid-Willamette Valley Community Action)[8]. Portland residents have additional options through Community Energy Project[5]
offices_or_providers: Local Community Action Agencies (CAAs)[1]
Senior centers[1]
Housing authorities[1]
Homes for Good (Lane County)[7]
Mid-Willamette Valley Community Action[8]
Community Energy Project (Portland)[5]

programs.official_source: Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) - Energy Services Programs. State plan based on 10 CFR Part 440 and 2 CFR Part 200[2]
gotchas: Income is calculated as GROSS income before any deductions — this is a common misunderstanding[1]
Renters can apply, but must check with their landlord before weatherization work begins[5]
If you earn above 200% of poverty level, you may qualify for the separate 'Savings Within Reach' program from Energy Trust of Oregon instead[5]
The program operates on a waiting list basis — there is no guarantee of immediate service[1]
An energy audit is required to determine which specific services will be provided; not all services are provided to every household[7]
For newly legalized aliens, there may be temporary disqualification from receiving weatherization benefits[2]
Dispute resolution process exists if application is denied — applicants may be entitled to review under Oregon Administrative Rules[3]
The program prioritizes certain populations (seniors, people with disabilities, families with young children); non-priority households may face longer wait times

The program operates through a network of local administrators rather than a single state office, creating regional variation in application processes and wait times. Priority tiers affect service timeline but not eligibility. No asset limits are specified in available documentation.

## Key Points for Your Guide

**For an elderly loved one specifically:** Seniors age 60 and older receive **priority status**, meaning they may be served faster than non-priority households[1]. However, there is no age requirement to qualify — the program serves all income-eligible households.

**Income verification is critical:** The program uses gross household income (before deductions), and all household members' income counts[1]. This often surprises applicants who think only the primary earner's income matters.

**The application process is local:** You cannot apply directly to the state. You must contact your county's Community Action Agency[5]. The search results provide specific phone numbers for five Oregon counties, but if your county isn't listed, contact Oregon Housing and Community Services to find your local administrator.

**Services are customized:** The program doesn't provide a standard package. After you qualify, an energy audit determines which improvements are most cost-effective for your specific home[1][7]. This means two households might receive different services.

**Waitlists are real:** The program explicitly mentions placing households on waiting service lists[1]. The search results don't specify typical wait times, which varies by region and current demand.

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Benefits Assistance (SHIBA)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of income.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no asset test applies.
other_requirements: Must have Medicare
Oregon residents
Available to people over 65 and people with disabilities ages 18-64

Provided by trained volunteer counselors via phone, in-person, online, public events, and one-on-one sessions.

methods: Phone: 800-722-4134 (toll-free statewide hotline; enter zip code for local routing)
Website: https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/helpnearyou.aspx (find local counseling sites)
Email: shiba.oregon@odhs.oregon.gov or online contact form at https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/connect-with-us.aspx
In-person: Local offices and events (search by zip code on site; examples: Clackamas 503-655-8269, Multnomah 503-988-3646)
Public events and presentations (check local calendars)
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling available by phone or appointment; no formal processing as it's direct service.
forms: No specific application forms required for service; volunteer application exists but not for beneficiaries
documents: Medicare card and insurance details recommended for counseling sessions, but not required to start

geography.restrictions: None; available to Oregon residents statewide.
geography.regional_variations: Local offices and events vary by county (e.g., different phone numbers like Clackamas 503-655-8269, Multnomah 503-988-3646); counselors routed by zip code; events hosted locally at libraries/offices.
offices_or_providers: Statewide network via https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/helpnearyou.aspx; Department of Consumer and Business Services, 350 Winter Street NE Room 330, Salem OR 97309-0405

programs.name: Family Caregiver Assistance Program (FCAP) / Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP)

eligibility.income_limits: No greater than 400% of the Federal Poverty Level: $4,163 per month for an individual, $5,637 per month for a couple. (Note: Figures from 2022; current FPL may differ. No full household size table provided.)[1]
eligibility.asset_limits: No greater than the average cost of six months in a nursing facility. Specific dollar amount not stated; primary residence and typical exempt assets (e.g., one vehicle) likely apply per Medicaid rules, but details not specified.[1]
other_requirements: Currently living in their own home (not provider-controlled facility, foster, or care home);[1]
Meets Oregon’s Service Priority Level 1 through 18;[1]
Exempt from estate recovery;[1]
Does not utilize other Medicaid services and supports;[1]
For FCSP: Caregiver 18+ caring for person 60+ or with Alzheimer’s/dementia (any age); or parent/relative 55+ caring for disabled adult 18+ or child 18- (Multnomah-specific example).[3][7]

benefits.value: Services and supports totaling no more than $500 per month, including: in-home support services or personal care services; adult day services (non-residential community-based programs).[1] FCSP offers broader supports like respite, counseling, training (varies by local program).[6][7]

methods: Contact Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC): 1-855-ORE-ADRC (673-2372) or www.ADRCofOregon.org for options counseling and eligibility screening;[7]
Oregon Department of Human Services (DHS) local office (for Medicaid-related);[4]
Online via Oregon DHS website (for Medicaid eligibility);[4]
application.waitlist: Not specified; funding-limited under Older Americans Act for FCSP.[6]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; uses DHS Medicaid application process and CAPS assessment for functional need in related programs.[2]
documents: Income statements, asset proof, ID, proof of home residency (e.g., utilities, rent/mortgage for 3+ months in related programs); medical/functional need documentation.[2][4]

geography.restrictions: Administered locally via Area Agencies on Aging (AAA) or ADRCs; care recipient must live in own home statewide.[1][7]
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered through local AAAs or providers; eligibility screening and specific services may vary by county (e.g., Multnomah County examples).[3][6]
offices_or_providers: Local Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) offices; Multnomah County FCSP as example.[3][7]

gotchas: Two distinct programs: FCAP (Medicaid-funded, strict income/asset limits, $500/mo cap) vs. FCSP (OAA-funded, supports for caregivers, less financial testing);[1][6]
Cannot use other Medicaid services; lives in own home only (no foster/assisted living);[1][2]
Proposed 1115 waiver status (2022 info) may affect rollout/availability;[1]
Income/assets based on 2022 FPL; verify current limits;[1]
Priority levels 1-18 required for FCAP.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Distinguishes FCAP (Medicaid, financial limits, fixed $500/mo) from FCSP (support services, caregiver-focused, local delivery); income at 400% FPL (high threshold); asset tied to nursing facility cost; priority-tiered access; statewide but locally administered.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income must not exceed 125% of the federal poverty level[3][4]. The federal poverty guidelines were updated as of January 15, 2025[4], but specific dollar amounts by household size are not provided in available sources. Families should contact their local provider or use the federal poverty guidelines calculator to determine their exact threshold.
other_requirements: Must be unemployed[3][4]

Enrollment priority given to: veterans and qualified spouses, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, those with low literacy skills or limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless or at-risk individuals, those with low employment prospects, and those who have exhausted American Job Center services[4]

benefits.value: Part-time transitional employment paying the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage. Participants work an average of 20 hours per week[4]. Program provides job training, skill updates, mentorship, and placement assistance into unsubsidized permanent employment[1][2][3]

methods: Phone: 503-228-5100 (Oregon Easter Seals)[1]
Phone: 541-687-7393 (Eugene/Lane County location)[2]
Phone: 1-877-US2-JOBS (1-877-872-5627) - national toll-free helpline[4]
Email: kgerhards@or.easterseals.com (referral inquiries)[1]
In-person: 2510 Oakmont Way, Eugene, OR 97401[2]
Online: CareerOneStop's Older Worker Program Finder (to locate local programs)[4]
Referral form available through Oregon Easter Seals[1]
forms: Referral form (available through Oregon Easter Seals)[1]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources. Contact local provider for required documentation.

geography.restrictions: Program operates statewide in Oregon through Easter Seals Oregon[1][5]
geography.regional_variations: Easter Seals operates SCSEP in multiple states including Oregon, Idaho, Alabama, Arizona, Illinois, New Jersey, New York, Oklahoma, Ohio, and Utah[5]. Within Oregon, at least one dedicated office is documented in Eugene (Lane County)[2]. Families should contact their local Easter Seals office for regional specifics.
offices_or_providers: Easter Seals Oregon (statewide administrator)[1][5]
Eugene location: 2510 Oakmont Way, Eugene, OR 97401[2]
Program partners with community-based non-profit organizations and government agencies as host agencies[2][3]

gotchas: Income limit is strict: 125% of federal poverty level, which is significantly below median income for most seniors[3][4]. Families must verify current poverty guidelines (updated January 15, 2025) to determine eligibility[4]
Program is transitional employment, not permanent placement — the goal is to bridge to unsubsidized jobs, not provide ongoing employment[3][4]
Participants must be actively unemployed; those with any current employment may not qualify[3][4]
Part-time work (average 20 hours/week) means income is supplemental, not full-time replacement[4]
Priority enrollment system means low-income seniors without additional risk factors (disability, age 65+, veteran status, etc.) may face longer wait times[4]
Program is federally funded and authorized by the Older Americans Act, so funding and availability may fluctuate with federal appropriations[3][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: SCSEP is a transitional employment program with strict income-based eligibility (125% federal poverty level) and priority enrollment tiers. Benefits are fixed (minimum wage, ~20 hours/week) rather than scaled. The program operates statewide in Oregon through Easter Seals as the primary administrator, with multiple host agencies providing actual job placements. Critical data gaps: specific processing times, waitlist status, required documentation, and current dollar amounts for income limits (which depend on federal poverty guidelines updated January 2025). Families must contact local providers for region-specific details.

**Key Takeaways for Families:**

The SCSEP is a **federally funded transitional employment program**, not a permanent benefits program. It's designed to help low-income seniors (55+) gain work experience and skills while earning minimum wage, with the explicit goal of moving them into permanent, unsubsidized jobs[3][4].

**The income test is the primary barrier.** At 125% of federal poverty level, most seniors will not qualify unless they have very limited income[3][4]. Families should verify the exact threshold using the 2025 federal poverty guidelines before applying.

**Contact Easter Seals Oregon directly** at 503-228-5100 or kgerhards@or.easterseals.com to confirm current eligibility, processing times, and required documentation—these details are not publicly specified in available sources[1].

programs.name: Legal Aid Services of Oregon (LASO)

eligibility.income_limits: For general LASO services, income up to 125% of federal poverty guidelines (varies by household size; exact dollar amounts not specified in sources—call for current table). For seniors (60+), services in many regional offices and projects are provided regardless of income or not subject to standard financial criteria.[1][2][3][6][10]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned for senior services.[1][2]
other_requirements: Low-income priority for full representation, but seniors often exempt from financial eligibility[1][2][3]
U.S. citizen or legal resident (Multnomah County exception for domestic violence victims)[6]
Residency in served county/region for senior projects[1][2][6]

benefits.value: Free 30-minute consultations with volunteer attorneys on civil issues (wills, powers of attorney, housing, consumer/debt, abuse protection, public benefits, family law, conservatorships/guardianships, nursing homes); brief services, advice, or full representation for low-income seniors in priority areas.[1][2][3][6]

methods: Phone (regional examples: Portland senior centers (503) 224-2640 or (503) 244-5204[1]; Albany (541) 926-8678[2]; Central Oregon (541) 385-6944 or 1-800-678-6944[3]; statewide intake varies)

In-person at senior centers (e.g., Multnomah County locations like Friendly House, Neighborhood House[1]; Central Oregon Regional Office, 42 NW Greeley Ave, Bend[3]; Albany office, 433 Fourth Ave SW, Albany[2])
No general online application specified; contact regional offices
application.processing_time: Intake hours Monday-Thursday 10am-12pm and 1-4pm; consultations scheduled via phone/in-person[1][3]
forms: No specific form names/numbers listed
documents: Not explicitly listed; prepare income/household details if low-income, proof of age/residency for seniors[3]

geography.restrictions: Regional offices serve specific counties (e.g., Multnomah[1][6], Benton/Linn[2], Central Oregon[3]); priority to greatest social/economic need[3][5]
geography.regional_variations: Services differ by office: e.g., Portland Senior Law Project offers income-blind 30-min meetings at senior centers[1]; Albany exempts seniors from financial criteria for select cases[2]; Central Oregon prioritizes low-income in housing/abuse/benefits with volunteer lawyers[3]
offices_or_providers: Portland Regional Office - Senior Law Project (Multnomah County senior centers)[1]
Albany Regional Office (Benton/Linn Counties)[2]
Central Oregon Regional Office (Bend)[3]

gotchas: Seniors often get income-blind initial consultations, but full representation prioritizes low-income (≤125% FPL)[1][2][10]
Services vary significantly by region/office—must contact local office[1][2][3]
Not criminal law; civil only (housing, benefits, abuse, etc.)[1][2][7]
Volunteer lawyers may charge for work beyond free consultation if retained[1][3]
U.S. citizen/legal resident required in some areas[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Seniors (60+) frequently exempt from standard income limits; regional offices with unique senior projects; no fixed dollar tables or asset tests specified; priority areas under Older Americans Act influence services

programs.name: Long-term Care Ombudsman

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; available to all residents regardless of financial status.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests; no assets count or are exempt as there is no financial eligibility.
other_requirements: Must be a resident of a licensed long-term care facility in Oregon (e.g., nursing homes, assisted living facilities, adult foster homes, board and care homes, hospice centers, adult day care).
Seniors or people with disabilities in such facilities.
No age minimum for residents; program serves targeted population of seniors and people with disabilities.

benefits.value: Complaint investigation and resolution; advocacy for residents' rights; information on residents' rights, abuse prevention, care planning, and navigating long-term care; regular facility visits and observation of care conditions; protection of individual rights, promotion of dignity, and improvement of care quality in facilities. No financial aid, hours per week, or dollar amounts provided.

methods: Phone: Contact local ombudsman via statewide program (specific numbers available through Oregon Law Help or local Area Agency on Aging; no central number in results).
In-person: Visit long-term care facility for on-site ombudsman representatives or local offices.
Online: Referrals via https://oregonlawhelp.org/referrals/oregon-long-term-care-ombudsman or state resources.
Family or resident can file complaints directly with ombudsman representatives at facilities or through the program.
application.processing_time: Not specified; complaint investigations handled promptly by ombudsman representatives.
forms: No specific forms required; complaints filed verbally, by phone, or in writing to ombudsman.
documents: No documents needed for service access; resident consent required for investigations if not filed by resident.

geography.restrictions: Limited to residents in licensed long-term care facilities across Oregon; no county restrictions.
geography.regional_variations: Local ombudsman designees and screening committees operate regionally; volunteers coordinated through local offices (e.g., Central Oregon example in Bend, ZIP 97702); facility visits and support provided locally but program standardized statewide.
offices_or_providers: State Office of the Long-Term Care Ombudsman (under Oregon Secretary of State).
Local designees and volunteers statewide.
Regional contacts via Area Agencies on Aging.

gotchas: This is not a direct service or financial aid program for families or individuals outside facilities—it's advocacy only for current long-term care facility residents.
Requires resident consent for complaint investigations; family complaints need resident direction.
Not for people at risk of institutionalization but living independently—only facility residents.
Program relies on volunteers (16 hours/month commitment) and state staff; availability depends on local designee presence.
People often confuse it with paid care services or Medicaid—it's free advocacy, not healthcare or housing placement.
Volunteers must be 21+, pass background checks, and complete 48-hour training; not instant access.
programs.data_shape_notes: no income or asset test; eligibility tied strictly to residing in licensed long-term care facilities; statewide but delivered via local volunteer designees; advocacy-only with no quantified benefits like dollars or hours

programs.name: Elderly Rental Assistance Program (ERA)

eligibility.age_note: At least one household member must be 58 years or older[3][4]
eligibility.income_limits: Very low income: 50% of Area Median Income (AMI) or less[4]. Specific dollar amounts vary by household size and county; consult local program administrators for exact thresholds in your area[4]
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available program documentation
eligibility.housing_status_requirement: Household must be homeless or unstably housed[3]
other_requirements: Oregon residency is not required; duration of residency and immigrant status are not considered in eligibility[3][4]
Citizenship is not required[4]
Must be assessed to determine eligibility; assessment costs are eligible expenses even if applicant is ultimately deemed ineligible[4]

benefits.value: Rental housing cost assistance; specific dollar amounts per household not detailed in available sources[3]
benefits.coverage: Program defays (pays for) the cost of rental housing[4]

methods: Contact local program administrators through Oregon Housing and Community Services Department (OHCS)
Phone: Specific ERA program phone number not provided in search results; contact OHCS for referral to local provider
In-person: Through local homeless prevention programs and Coordinated Entry System (CoC) partners[1]
application.forms: Specific form names/numbers not provided; documentation requirements detailed in SHF Manual[3]
documents: Proof of income (general, zero, self-employed, or non-traditional income)[1]
Self-attestation permitted as last resort if formal documentation unavailable[1]
Proof of Oregon residency (though residency duration is not a criterion)[1]
Documentation of homeless or unstably housed status

geography.restrictions: Program serves all of Oregon; no county-specific restrictions noted[3]
geography.regional_variations: Program is administered locally through homeless prevention programs and Coordinated Entry System partners, which may vary by region[1]. Processing times and specific benefit amounts may vary by local provider capacity.
geography.offices_or_providers: Administered through local homeless prevention programs coordinated with Coordinated Entry System (CoC); specific office locations not listed in available sources

programs.official_source: https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=3636 (OAR 813-053); Oregon Housing and Community Services Department (OHCS)
gotchas: Age threshold is 58, not 65—many seniors assume 65 is the cutoff[3][4]
Program specifically targets unstably housed or homeless elderly; stable housing does not qualify[3]
Income limit is 50% AMI, which is significantly lower than many other assistance programs (e.g., some programs go up to 80% AMI)[4]
No citizenship or residency duration requirement, but applicants may encounter local agencies with stricter policies—clarify with your local provider[4]
Assessment is required for all applicants; even if deemed ineligible, the assessment cost is a covered expense[4]
Specific dollar amounts and benefit caps are not publicly detailed in regulatory documents; families must contact local providers for exact benefit amounts
Program manual (SHF Manual) contains specific eligibility documentation requirements but is not directly linked in public sources—request from local provider[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: ERA is a decentralized program administered through local homeless prevention agencies rather than a single state office. Benefits and processing vary by local provider. The program has no citizenship or residency requirements, which is unusual among housing assistance programs. Income limits are tied to Area Median Income (AMI), which varies by county, so families must determine their local AMI threshold. The program is specifically designed for prevention of homelessness among elderly households, not general rental assistance.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Start here:** Contact your local Coordinated Entry System (CoC) or homeless prevention agency in your county[1]. They administer ERA locally and can provide exact income limits for your area, current benefit amounts, and processing timelines.

**Critical eligibility points:** Your loved one must be at least 58 years old[4], have income at or below 50% AMI[4], and be either homeless or unstably housed[3]. Citizenship and length of Oregon residency do not matter[3][4].

**What "unstably housed" means:** The program targets people at immediate risk of homelessness, not those in stable housing situations. This is the key distinction from general rental assistance programs.

**Income verification:** If your loved one lacks traditional income documentation, self-attestation is permitted as a last resort[1]—this is important for seniors with irregular income sources.

**No published benefit amounts:** Unlike some programs, ERA does not publish specific dollar amounts per household size. You must contact your local provider to learn what assistance is available in your area.

programs.name: Oregon Senior Free Tuition Audit Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits apply. This program has no financial eligibility requirements.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits apply. No financial tests are required.
other_requirements: Must be an Oregon resident.
Space must be available in the course after tuition-paying students have registered.
Instructor or department approval required.
Limited to auditing (no grade or credit earned).
Maximum of 8 credits per term.
Lower-division collegiate courses only (varies by institution; excludes limited entry programs like nursing or aquarium science).

benefits.value: 100% tuition waiver for auditing credit classes (up to 8 credits per term). Other fees (course fees, technology fees, etc.) must be paid. No credit or grade earned.

methods: Varies by college: Contact the specific community college or public university (e.g., Oregon Coast Community College: email instructor and Registrar@OregonCoast.edu; Portland Community College: submit online Senior audit discount request form after admission; Clatsop CC: present proof of age).

General steps: 1) Apply/admit to the college if new student. 2) Find class in schedule. 3) Get instructor permission. 4) Submit senior waiver/audit request form. 5) Register after first day if space available.
application.processing_time: Varies by institution; registration occurs after first day of class if approved (e.g., first week of term confirmation at OCCC).
application.waitlist: No formal waitlist; space-available only after paying students register.
forms: Senior Waiver form (Oregon Coast CC)
Senior audit discount request (Portland CC)
Institution-specific forms or proof of age
documents: Proof of age (e.g., ID showing 65+)
Proof of Oregon residency
Admission application if new to college

geography.restrictions: Available at Oregon community colleges and public universities, but specifics vary by institution.
geography.regional_variations: Age threshold and discounts vary: some colleges (e.g., Portland CC, Oregon Coast CC) allow from age 62 with discounts/audit; others (e.g., Clatsop CC, OSU, UO) strictly 65+ for full tuition waiver. Additional discounts like Gold Card (25% off from age 62 at Clatsop). Application processes and exclusions differ.
offices_or_providers: Community colleges (e.g., Oregon Coast CC, Clatsop CC, Portland CC)
Public universities (e.g., Oregon State University, University of Oregon)

programs.official_source: https://www.oregonlegislature.gov/bills_laws/ors/ors341.html (ORS 341.518 for community colleges); https://www.oregonlegislature.gov/bills_laws/ors/ors352.html (ORS 352.303 for public universities)
gotchas: No credit or grade earned—purely audit for personal enrichment.
Space not guaranteed; only after paying students.
Fees still apply (not just tuition).
Instructor approval required; email in advance.
Excludes limited-entry programs (e.g., nursing, aquarium science).
Some colleges start benefits at 62 with partial discounts, not full waiver until 65.
Must register after first day of class per state rules.
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide statutory framework (ORS 341.518/352.303), but implemented differently by each college/university with varying age cutoffs (62-65), processes, and minor discounts; no income/asset test; institution-specific exclusions and max credits.

programs.name: Health Related Social Needs Benefits (HSRN)

eligibility.income_limits: For housing benefits (rent/utilities, storage, tenancy): Household income 30% or less of Area Median Income (AMI) where you live, plus lack resources/support to prevent homelessness. Varies by location (e.g., county-specific AMI tables not detailed in sources; check local Fair Market Rent data). No general income limit stated for all HRSN; OHP Medicaid eligibility applies broadly.[1][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No specific asset limits mentioned; for housing, must 'lack resources or support networks to prevent homelessness'.[1][3][4]
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in Oregon Health Plan (OHP) Medicaid.[1][2][3][4][5][6]

In at least one qualifying life transition: release from incarceration past 12 months; discharge from Institution for Mental Disease (IMD) past 12 months; current/past Oregon child welfare involvement; transitioning to dual Medicaid/Medicare eligibility (next 3 months or past 9 months); homeless or at risk of homelessness; youth 19-26 with special health care needs (starting 2025).[1][2][3][5][6]

Plus clinical risk factors for specific services: complex physical/behavioral health condition, developmental/intellectual disability, difficulty with self-care/ADLs, experiencing abuse/neglect, age 65+, under 6, pregnant/postpartum, high crisis service use, etc.[1][2][3][4][5]
Cannot receive same/similar benefit from other state/local/federal programs.[6]

benefits.value: Housing: rent/utilities up to 6 months, storage fees, medically necessary home modifications (e.g., wheelchair ramps, grab bars), remediation (pest control, deep cleaning), hotel costs if needed to stay housed. Nutrition: medically tailored meals for low/very low food security (assessed by dietitian/PCP). Climate: support for extreme weather events. Must improve health condition; specific to social needs like food, housing, climate.[3][4][5][6]

methods: Contact healthcare provider or local OHP community partner.[3]
CCO-specific: PacificSource request form (via 211info follow-up); Health Share, AllCare, Advanced Health, IHN-CCO via their sites/providers.[2][4][5][6]
Unite Us Assistance Request Form (referral).[6]
OHP Open Card: care coordination.[7]
Check OHP status: 1-800-273-0557 or local ODHS.[6]
No central statewide form; varies by CCO/provider.
application.processing_time: Varies by service/provider; high volume causes longer times (e.g., Lane County PacificSource backlog as of Jan 2026); reviewed in order received.[4]
application.waitlist: Possible due to volume/provider capacity; times vary.[1][4]
forms: Unite Us Assistance Request Form[6]
PacificSource HRSN request form[4]
No single statewide form number
documents: Proof of OHP enrollment.
Lease/written landlord agreement (for housing).
Income verification (for housing: showing ≤30% AMI).
Medical documentation of clinical risk (via provider assessment).
Food security assessment (nutrition).[4]

geography.restrictions: Available statewide to OHP members via 16 CCOs or Open Card, but delivery via regional CCOs/providers.[1][2][4][5][6][7]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by CCOs (e.g., IHN-CCO, PacificSource Lane County backlog, Health Share, AllCare, Advanced Health); AMI for income varies by county; wait times/provider availability differ.[2][4][5]
offices_or_providers: Regional CCOs (e.g., PacificSource, Health Share of Oregon, AllCare Health, Advanced Health, IHN-CCO); local OHP partners; 211info for some; Community Action Agencies for emergencies.[1][4]

gotchas: Not for all OHP members—strict federal life transition + clinical risk requirements; not for emergencies (use CAA instead).[1][3]
Housing requires current housing/lease + ≤30% AMI + lack of resources—not for unhoused without path to housing.[3][4]
Varies by CCO; don't resubmit across CCOs (e.g., Lane County).[4]
Must not duplicate other program benefits.[6]
Processing delays common; work with provider first.[1][3][4]
OHP enrollment prerequisite—apply separately if needed.[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: OHP Medicaid-restricted; eligibility ties to life transitions + clinical risks per service; income/AMI only for housing sub-benefits; CCO-administered with regional providers/wait variations; no age cap but specific age risks (65+, <6); scales by service type and local AMI.
